does: This paper proposes Energy-Based Decoding (EBD), a training-free, reward-guided decoding framework that modifies how pre-trained language models generate outputs without updating their parameters. EBD augments the standard decoding process with an external lightweight reward model that steers generation toward high-utility responses while maintaining fidelity to the pre-trained model's prior distribution. The method combines energy-based modeling principles with reward signals to reshape output distributions during inference, enabling base models to produce task-oriented, well-formed answers across both open-ended and objective-driven tasks. The authors demonstrate that EBD can unlock instruction-following and task-appropriate behavior from frozen pre-trained models more efficiently and robustly than existing decoding-only approaches.
connects: This work builds on a long line of research addressing the gap between next-token prediction objectives and task performance in language models. It extends recent decoding-only methods that attempt to reshape output distributions without retraining, such as those using contrastive decoding or distribution distillation, but identifies their brittleness across diverse tasks. The paper connects to broader work on inference-time control of LLMs, including constrained decoding and guided generation approaches. It also draws on the classical energy-based model literature and reward-augmented generation techniques. Fundamentally, it responds to the recognition that post-training (via RLHF or instruction-tuning) is expensive, and that evaluation of pre-trained models is confounded by decoding artifacts—motivations emerging from recent discussions about separating true model capabilities from training-induced behaviors.
enables: This work enables more accurate evaluation of pre-trained LLM capabilities by isolating model competence from decoding failures, facilitating fairer benchmark comparisons without expensive post-training. The training-free nature of EBD suggests pathways toward generalizable, lightweight inference-time intervention methods that could work across diverse model architectures and task types. Future research could explore scaling reward models for different task families, combining multiple reward signals for complex objectives, or integrating EBD with other inference-time techniques. The framework also opens directions for studying which behaviors are inherent to pre-trained representations versus artifacts of optimization procedures, and could inform more efficient model adaptation strategies that minimize computational overhead during deployment.
